data.title: Sherwin-Williams outlines $10.70–$11.10 EPS target for 2026 while reinstating 401(k) match amid persistent demand uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sherwin-Williams (SHW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Heidi Petz, Chairman, CEO & President, highlighted that Sherwin-Williams delivered “record full year consolidated sales and record adjusted diluted earnings per share” in 2025, with “gross profit dollars and gross margin expanded. Adjusted EBITDA dollars and adjusted EBITDA margin also expanded.” Petz emphasized the company’s focus on share gains, targeted investments, and execution on enterprise priorities despite a prolonged challenging demand environment.
  • Management reported that Sherwin-Williams completed the Suvinil acquisition, returned $2.5 billion to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends, and opened its new global headquarters and technology center. Petz stated, “We completed the acquisition of Suvinil, and we continue to make strategic CapEx investments, including our new global headquarters and global technology center.”
  • Petz announced the reinstatement and retroactive restoration of the company’s 401(k) matching program for U.S. employees, explaining, “We chose to protect jobs, retain talent and invest in the long-term health of the organization by keeping our teams intact.”
  • CFO Benjamin Meisenzahl noted, “Adjusted diluted net income per share in the quarter increased by 6.7%. Adjusted EBITDA in the quarter grew 13.4% and expanded 120 basis points to 17.7% as a percent of sales. Free cash flow conversion in the quarter was 90.1%.”

Outlook

  • Management guided for 2026 consolidated sales to be up a low to mid-single-digit percentage, with diluted net income per share expected in the range of $10.70 to $11.10. Adjusted diluted net income per share is expected to be $11.50 to $11.90, “an increase of 2.4% at the midpoint compared to 2025’s adjusted diluted net income per share of $11.43,” according to Petz.
  • The Paint Stores Group implemented a 7% price increase effective January 1, with expected realization “in the low single-digit range given market dynamics and segment mix.” Management expects raw materials to be “up a low single-digit percentage in 2026, driven by tariffs along with select commodities also inflating.”
  • The 2026 environment is anticipated to remain challenging, with Petz stating, “The softer for longer dynamic we described again back in October remains intact. While some conditions are gradually becoming more stable, many of the indicators we track, along with cautious consumer sentiment, provide little support for any broad-based or accelerated recovery at this time.”
  • Sherwin-Williams expects to open 80 to 100 net new stores in the U.S. and Canada and will recommend an annual dividend increase of 1.3% to $3.20 per share.

Financial Results

  • In Q4, consolidated sales increased by a mid-single-digit percentage, with the Suvinil acquisition contributing a low single-digit percentage. Petz reported, “Gross margin was flattish year-over-year, but expanded excluding the dilutive impact of the Suvinil acquisition.”
  • Paint Stores Group sales grew as expected, led by high single-digit growth in Protective & Marine, while residential repaint “remained solid” but was “just slightly below the mid-single-digit range.” Segment margin expanded 90 basis points to 20.8%.
  • Consumer Brands Group sales exceeded expectations, driven by Suvinil and positive FX, with underlying business sales “essentially flat, which was better than we expected and drove the top line beat.”
  • Performance Coatings Group sales were at the high end of expectations, with packaging and Auto Refinish leading, and adjusted segment margin improved 150 basis points to 19%.
  • Free cash flow for the year was $2.7 billion and free cash flow conversion was 59%.

Q&A

  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird, asked about Performance Coatings margins; Petz responded that the results reflected “discipline on display,” highlighting aggressive focus on new business and simplification. CFO Meisenzahl added, “SG&A has been a focus of this team here. They've consistently been able to keep their SG&A at a moderated pace considering where volumes are at.”
  • John McNulty, BMO, inquired about SG&A outlook; Meisenzahl clarified, “SG&A up a low single digit. That obviously includes the history of the restructuring costs that we took in 2025…closer to a $46 million in savings in 2026.”
  • Christopher Parkinson, Wolfe Research, asked about gross margin and cost dynamics; Jaye explained, “We're guiding to our raw material basket to be up a low single-digit percentage this year,” noting pressure on packaging, pigments, and resins. Meisenzahl highlighted, “Healthcare up double digits. We're in that camp. We have things that we can do to mitigate that.”
  • Gregory Melich, Evercore, queried about price/mix realization; Petz stated, “We're going to continue to be, as you would expect, extremely aggressive as it relates to chasing volume right now. And so there's a balance.”
  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank, asked about weather impact; Meisenzahl responded, “We have weather every quarter. I mean, it impacts us every year…no concerns there right now.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was cautious, with recurring questions about margin sustainability, cost management, and demand outlook. Multiple inquiries probed the realism of guidance and pricing strategies.
  • Management struck a confident and disciplined tone in prepared remarks but shifted to a more defensive stance during Q&A, emphasizing control over costs and market share initiatives. Petz frequently reiterated confidence in the team’s ability to outperform, while Meisenzahl used phrases like “we're going to continue to find ways despite what's happening in the market.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains pragmatic and focused on execution, with little change in the overall cautious optimism despite persistent end-market challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is now more detailed, with a specific adjusted EPS range provided ($11.50–$11.90), compared to a narrowed range in Q3 with a midpoint of $11.35. Management continues to expect a “softer for longer” demand environment but signals readiness to outperform guidance if market conditions improve.
  • The company has restored its 401(k) match, reversing the temporary pause announced in Q3, citing a less severe realization of certain risks.
  • Analysts remain focused on volume and pricing trade-offs, margin sustainability, and the impact of macro headwinds. The management team continues to stress discipline, cost control, and aggressive pursuit of share gains.
  • Management’s confidence in navigating challenging conditions is consistent with previous quarters, though there is an increased emphasis on capitalizing on disruption and maintaining execution capacity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing risks from “muted CapEx spending, high rates, and affordability challenges.” Petz noted, “many of the indicators we track, along with cautious consumer sentiment, provide little support for any broad-based or accelerated recovery at this time.”
  • Tariff impacts are acknowledged, with delayed realization, and the company expects raw material and healthcare costs to rise.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the sustainability of margin improvements and the balance between price and volume in a competitive environment. Management stressed its focus on “controlling what we can control.”

Final Takeaway

Sherwin-Williams management maintains a disciplined approach in the face of persistent end-market softness, targeting 2026 adjusted EPS of $11.50–$11.90 and low to mid-single-digit sales growth. Strategic priorities include aggressive share gain initiatives, continued cost control, and restored employee benefits, with confidence in the company’s ability to outperform if market conditions improve. The leadership team remains focused on driving returns and expanding its footprint, while monitoring macroeconomic challenges and competitive dynamics closely.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Axis Capital outlines mid- to high single-digit premium growth for 2026 while investing in technology and new business lines
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AXIS Capital Holdings Limited (AXS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Vincent Tizzio highlighted "13 quarters of increases in diluted book value per share growth and 77% growth over that period." He emphasized a "18% year-over-year increase in diluted book value per common share at $77.20, 18% operating return on equity, record gross written premiums of $9.6 billion, up 7% over the prior year and a combined ratio of 89.8%, our lowest full year combined ratio since 2010." Tizzio pointed to momentum from strategic investments: "We leaned into attractive specialty markets, drove increased profitable growth that was largely propelled by our new and expanded business classes and further enhanced our operating efficiency."
  • The CEO stated, "We accelerated these efforts in 2025 and indeed raised our investment threshold with an emphasis on scaling our new and expanded lines and integrating a new AI-enabled front end within our organization."
  • Regarding the Insurance segment, Tizzio reported "record gross written premiums of $7.2 billion, a 9% increase over the prior year, record new premiums written of $2.4 billion, record underwriting income of $597 million, a 40% increase over the prior year."
  • In North America, premiums rose 10%, attributed to "strategic investments in product and channel expansion, coupled with significant underwriting platform enhancements."
  • On the Reinsurance side, he noted, "AXIS Re has generated positive bottom line results for the last 8 consecutive quarters... a strong combined ratio of 92.6%, underwriting income of $128 million."
  • Tizzio described the introduction of AXIS Capacity Solutions (ACS), stating, "Through ACS, we are tapping into our deep knowledge and experience with third-party capital, bringing innovation to the insurance platform of our company... several transactions have already been completed."
  • CFO Peter Vogt stated, "Our net income available to common shareholders was $282 million or $3.67 per diluted common share. For the full year, $978 million or $12.35 per diluted common share, producing a 17% return on common equity."
  • Vogt added, "Our gross premiums written of $2.2 billion were up 12% over the prior year quarter, driven by accelerating growth initiatives in insurance. On a net basis, premiums were up 13%."

Outlook

  • Vogt reiterated, "As we look to 2026, given the current insurance market conditions, we reiterate our confidence that we can grow gross written premiums at a mid- to high single-digit rate while maintaining premium adequacy at our long-term targets."
  • He commented on reinsurance, "Our overall reinsurance gross premiums could be down in 2026, even up to double digits. However, while the volume may be reduced, we remain confident in the portfolio's expected underwriting profitability."
  • Vogt affirmed a focus on expense management: "We feel good about our glide path into 2026 and we're holding to our commitment of an 11% G&A ratio as we go into 2026."

Financial Results

  • Book value per diluted common share was reported as $77.20 at December 31, an increase of 18% over the past 12 months.
  • Operating income for the quarter was $250 million or $3.25 per diluted common share.
  • Gross premiums written for the quarter reached $2.2 billion, up 12% year-over-year. Full-year gross premiums were $9.6 billion, up 7%.
  • The quarterly combined ratio was 90.4%; for the full year, it was 89.8%.
  • Catastrophe losses in the quarter were $30 million, with a cat loss ratio of 2%.
  • Insurance expense ratios for the quarter and year were 33.4% and 31.6%, respectively.
  • Investment income was $187 million for the quarter and $767 million for the year.
  • Share repurchases totaled $888 million for the year, with an additional $112 million authorized.

Q&A

  • Andrew Kligerman, TD Cowen: Asked about the G&A ratio pathway and sustainability of the combined ratio. Vogt responded, "We feel good about our glide path into 2026 and we're holding to our commitment of an 11% G&A ratio as we go into 2026," and added, "all-in combined, around that 90% target is a pretty good number for the overall company."
  • Yaron Kinar, Mizuho: Probed on expense ratio sustainability and willingness to invest in new talent. Tizzio agreed, "We feel very good, again, about the ability to monetize the numerous investments that we took in our operating platform."
  • Christian Trost, Wells Fargo: Inquired about insurance growth drivers and ACS impact. Tizzio explained, "about $150-odd million of our growth came from the new and expanded classes."
  • Jing Li, KBW: Sought details on lower middle market growth and core loss ratio trends. Tizzio described "the continued proposition development that our North American team has created... We're pleased with the sustained growth."
  • Joshua Shanker, BofA: Asked about third-party underwriting. Tizzio noted, "delegated across our insurance platform represent approximately 32% of the volume of what we produced in the company."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a slightly positive tone, focusing on growth sustainability, expense discipline, and capital deployment, with recurring questions about the G&A ratio and margin sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing achievement of targets and discipline, with Tizzio stating, "We remain focused on continuing to pursue our specialty leadership ambition while delivering tailored insurance solutions to our customers and producing attractive returns for our shareholders."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone in Q4 remained confident, while analysts continued to probe on cost control and growth drivers but showed some increased attention to capital deployment and market competitiveness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management guidance on gross written premium growth for insurance remains at "mid- to high single-digit" for 2026, consistent with Q3, with RAC Re potentially lifting growth into double digits.
  • The focus on expense discipline and a target G&A ratio of 11% carried over from the prior quarter, but Q4 included more detail on realizing operational leverage from technology investments.
  • Combined ratio performance remained a highlight, with Q4 emphasizing sustainability near the 90% level.
  • Analysts maintained focus on growth mix, capital return, and cost management. Management’s confidence in new and expanded lines and ACS was reiterated and further detailed.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted competitive pressure in property and the challenging environment in cyber, with Tizzio stating, "We will continue to maintain a cautious and selective appetite and do not see cyber as a growth area for the foreseeable future unless a better risk-reward outcome is realized."
  • Vogt noted, "If we continue to see a challenging reinsurance market as we progress through 2026, we will remain bottom line focused. Our overall reinsurance gross premiums could be down in 2026, even up to double digits."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of expense ratios, margin pressures from talent costs, and the potential for variability in capital returns.

Final Takeaway

AXIS Capital closed 2025 with robust growth in book value, underwriting income, and gross premiums, while strengthening its operating platform through technology investments and new business lines. Management reaffirmed its targets for mid- to high single-digit premium growth in insurance for 2026 and remains committed to operational efficiency, with a clear focus on managing expense ratios and maintaining profitability. The company continues to emphasize a disciplined approach to underwriting, selective growth in specialty areas, and prudent capital management, positioning AXIS for continued momentum and strong shareholder returns in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: A. O. Smith signals 4% EPS growth for 2026 with $70M Leonard Valve acquisition while navigating China headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: A. O. Smith Corporation (AOS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Shafer highlighted that "2025 sales increased slightly as pricing benefits and higher commercial water heater and boiler volumes were offset by lower China sales." He noted a "record EPS" for the company, citing a 6% increase to $3.85 and improved segment profitability. Shafer discussed the completion of the Leonard Valve acquisition, stating, "This acquisition expands our water management market reach, digital capabilities and integrated product portfolio."
  • Shafer emphasized that North America water heater sales rose 1% in 2025, with commercial volumes offsetting wholesale residential softness due to new construction slowdown. He pointed out, "North America boiler sales grew 8% compared to 2024," and that water treatment sales decreased 2% as the company shifted focus from on-the-shelf retail to more profitable channels.
  • The CEO described China sales as down 12% in local currency, attributing this to economic weakness and discontinued government subsidies, but noted restructuring and expense management aided margin expansion.
  • CFO Charles Lauber reported, "We delivered sales of $3.8 billion in 2025, a slight increase over last year. 2025 earnings were $3.85 per share compared with adjusted earnings of $3.73 per share in 2024." He added, "North America segment earnings of $728 million increased 2% compared with 2024 adjusted segment earnings. Segment margin was 24.4%, an increase of 20 basis points year-over-year."
  • Lauber stated, "We generated strong free cash flow of $546 million during 2025, a 15% increase over 2024, primarily driven by lower year-over-year capital investments as well as higher earnings and the benefit of a onetime tax adjustment."

Outlook

  • The company projects a 2026 EPS range of $3.85 to $4.15 per share. Lauber said, "The midpoint of our EPS range represents 4% growth over our 2025 EPS."
  • A. O. Smith expects 2026 CapEx between $70 million and $80 million, free cash flow between $525 million and $575 million, and interest expense of $30 million to $40 million. The company plans to repurchase approximately $200 million of stock in 2026, with an approved 5 million additional shares.
  • Shafer stated, "We project that 2026 U.S. residential industry unit volumes will be flat to down compared to 2025," and expects U.S. commercial water heater industry volumes to "increase mid-single digits in 2026" ahead of regulatory changes. Boiler sales in North America are projected to grow 6% to 8%, and water treatment sales are expected to grow 10% to 12%. China sales are projected to decrease mid-single digits, with a challenging first half and expected recovery in the second half of 2026.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter 2025 sales were $913 million, flat year-over-year. Fourth quarter earnings were $0.90 per share, a 6% increase over the prior year's $0.85.
  • North America segment Q4 sales were $714 million, up 3% year-over-year. Segment margin was 23.1%, a 70 basis point improvement. Rest of the World segment Q4 sales were $206 million, down 13%, with a segment margin of 7.8% compared to 8.1% last year.
  • Free cash flow for 2025 was $546 million. The company returned $597 million to shareholders via dividends and repurchases, including $401 million in share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Jae Hyun Ko, Jefferies: Asked about persistent residential volume weakness. Lauber explained, "Where we're seeing some headwind is kind of the new home completions, multifamily and single family."
  • Ko, Jefferies: Inquired about China guidance and return to growth. Shafer said, "The return to growth will be partly driven by as we move past that phase and we get back to the remodel wins and the refurbishments that still need to happen in China."
  • Michael Halloran, Baird: Sought more context on wholesale channel competition. Shafer responded, "The dynamic of low new home construction...and the fact that retail has made some inroads in terms of share gains overall in the industry is just putting pressure on that part of the channel."
  • Mitchell Moore, KeyBanc: Asked about Leonard Valve's growth. Shafer said, "In terms of the end markets, they're very strong in kind of the commercial markets," and Lauber noted, "the business has been growing double digits."
  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: Questioned water management platform prospects. Shafer indicated the move expands opportunities and the company has a "healthy pipeline of where we could go to do that."
  • Scott Graham, Seaport: Probed on wholesale residential channel pressures. Shafer clarified, "I don't think the characterization of a jump ball is what's happening...the dynamics itself are not new."
  • Ethan Coyle, JPMorgan: Asked about scaling India. Shafer focused on leveraging the Pureit addition and ongoing innovation.
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Asked for clarification on steel price outlook. Lauber stated, "That 10% up is the year-over-year average."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious and probing sentiment, focusing on competitive pressures, China headwinds, and the sustainability of growth in India and water management. Questions often sought clarity on persistent challenges and margin outlook, indicating slight skepticism.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, highlighting margin improvements, strategic acquisitions, and confidence in long-term growth. Phrases such as "we are confident" and "we think we can continue" were used, balanced with recognition of ongoing challenges in China and the wholesale channel.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remains cautious, with similar focus areas. Management's tone has shifted slightly more confident, especially regarding strategic actions and portfolio expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from "flat to up 1%" sales growth in 2025 (Q3 call) to a projected 2% to 5% top-line growth for 2026.
  • Strategic focus has expanded with the Leonard Valve acquisition, moving deeper into water management. This marks a tangible step beyond the previous quarter's emphasis on water treatment and portfolio assessment.
  • Analysts' focus remains on China, residential volume challenges, and competitive dynamics; however, there is increased attention on the implications of the Leonard Valve acquisition and water management strategy.
  • Key metrics: EPS improved from $3.73 (2024) to $3.85 (2025); Q4 sales remained essentially flat year-over-year.
  • Management's confidence appears stronger, referencing concrete progress in restructuring, M&A, and operational improvements, while still acknowledging risk factors in China and new construction.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing softness in China due to "continued low consumer confidence, a discontinued government subsidy program and ongoing competitive intensity."
  • The company projects "input costs, our guidance assumes that steel prices in the full year 2026 will increase approximately 10% compared to 2025," and continued headwinds from tariffs.
  • There are headwinds in wholesale residential water heaters from new construction slowdown and increased retail competition.
  • Management is monitoring discretionary spend in water treatment and remains cautious about the pace of China recovery.

Final Takeaway

A. O. Smith closed 2025 with record EPS and improved margins, underpinned by growth in commercial water heater and boiler sales, and margin improvements in water treatment, even as China remained a significant headwind. The company’s 2026 guidance anticipates stable to modest growth, with a focus on broadening the portfolio through the Leonard Valve acquisition and continued investment in operational excellence. Management remains confident in its ability to drive profitable growth through strategic actions and disciplined cost control, while actively managing risks in China and the wholesale residential channel.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elon Musk's SpaceX, xAI in merger talks ahead of planned IPO - Reuters
data.description:
Elon Musk's SpaceX (SPACE) and xAI are in merger talks ahead of initial public offering later this year.

Under the proposed merger, shares of xAI would be exchanged for shares in SpaceX (SPACEX), according to a Reuters report on Thursday, which cited a person briefed on the matter and two recent company filings viewed by the news agency. Two entities have been set up in Nevada to facilitate the transaction.

A merger would combine Musk's rockets, Starlink satellites, the social media platform X and GrokAI chatbot under one roof, according to the report.

Some xAI executives could be given the option to receive cash instead of SpaceX (SPACE) stock as part of the deal, according to Reuters. A final agreement hasn't been signed and the timing and structure of a transaction remain fluid.

Musk, SpaceX, and xAI didn't respond to Reuters requests for comment. 

The Financial Times reported earlier that SpaceX is looking at a mid-June IPO, aiming to raise as much as  $50B at valuation of roughly $1.5T.

Tesla (TSLA) said on Wednesday that it will invest about $2B in xAI (X.AI).Tesla said that on Jan. 16 the company signed an agreement to invest about $2B to acquire shares of Series E Preferred Stock of xAI as part of their recent publicly-disclosed financing round.

data.title: Royal Caribbean charts a profitable course with new ships and disciplined costs
data.description:
Buoyed by a strong profit outlook for 2026, record bookings for the year, and expansion plans that include new ships, new destinations, and new European itineraries, shares of Royal Caribbean (RCL) were launched 15% higher on Thursday to their highest level in four months.

The revenue growth, however, was just half the story as Royal Caribbean (RCL) successfully controlled costs and navigated the saturated Caribbean market.

“Another year of flat to +1% of net cruise cost ex-fuel is decidedly better than expectations of UBS’s +2.0% estimate,” UBS analyst Robin Farley writes, with the cost profile also contributing to the full year EPS outlook of $17.70 to $18.10 per share.

Besides financials, Royal Caribbean’s fleet expansion is contributing to the stock’s performance with the upcoming addition of 10 new ships for its Celebrity River business, along with two new ocean ships.

“With this announcement, RCL grows into the highly fragmented European river market, with announced additional sailings on the Danube and the Rhine,” noted BofA Securities’ Andrew Didora.

The pressure from Caribbean oversaturation should be felt more acutely in the first quarter. But RCL’s yield growth guidance of 1.0% to 1.5% is “good enough and suggests RCL can still grow total pricing despite these headwinds,” says BNP Paribas analyst Xian Siew, adding that once the company is past some of the Caribbean headwinds, “pricing can reaccelerate with a path to 3%+.”

“With RCL’s growth story still intact and better-than-expected cost controls driving upside on full year EPS guidance, we think this should be a green light to cruise and remain Outperform,” Siew adds.

Royal Caribbean’s (RCL) upbeat outlook and outsized gain in the share price is lifting the segment, with shares of Carnival Corp (CCL), Norwegian Cruise Lines (NCLH), and Viking Holdings (VIK) all trading with gains of 5% to 8%.

data.title: Las Vegas Sands is defended by analysts amid its biggest share price decline since the pandemic
data.description:
Las Vegas Sands (LVS) was one of the worst-performing stocks in the S&P 500 Index on Thursday and was on pace in afternoon trading to have its worst day since the pandemic headlines hit. Investors were rattled after the casino company's earnings report threw a spotlight on margin pressure on Macau. 

Jefferies analyst David Katz highlighted that the continued growth of Marina Bay Sands and the successful ramp of The Londoner were overshadowed by lower-than-expected margins across the Macau portfolio. "We do not believe these results represent a structural shift in the market and continue to consider LVS a relative winner," advised Katz. The Jefferies view is that strong results during the Chinese New Year holiday could restore investor confidence in the growth algorithm for Buy-rated Las Vegas Sands (LVS).

CBRE Research analyst John DeCree also kept a Buy rating in place on Las Vegas Sands (LVS). DeCree said that while the company's performance in Macau has been underwhelming for the past couple of quarters, the roaring success of Marina Bay Sands should give investors plenty to stay excited about while LVS management recalibrates the strategy in Macau. CBRE hiked its price target on LVS to $72, which implies an 11.0X trading multiple to the firm's FY27 EBITDA estimate. "The main driver of our PT increase is the extension of our valuation year from FY26 to FY27, which we expect to include more growth across both Macau and MBS," updated DeCree. 

Shares of Las Vegas Sands (LVS) were down 13.6% at 2:08 p.m.

data.title: Bread Financial outlines low single-digit loan growth for 2026 as credit metrics improve
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bread Financial Holdings (BFH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ralph Andretta reported that Bread Financial delivered strong fourth quarter and full year 2025 results, highlighting "7 major new brand signings in 2025 and renewing multiple existing partners in a number of verticals." The home vertical expanded significantly with new relationships, including Bed Bath & Beyond, Furniture First, and Raymour & Flanigan. Andretta indicated the successful launch of partnerships with Crypto.com, Cricket Wireless, and Vivint, noting these demonstrate "how our product solutions span all generational segments and are supported by our digital-first approach."
  • Andretta stated, "All of our top 10 programs are now renewed into at least 2028," and cited the launch of an enhanced Caesars Rewards credit card as evidence of ongoing product innovation. Co-brand comprised 52% of credit sales in Q4, up from 48% in Q4 2024.
  • Direct-to-consumer deposit balances increased 11% year-over-year and have grown for 20 consecutive quarters, now representing 48% of average total funding in Q4, up from 43% a year ago. Capital return included $350 million to shareholders, with $310 million in share repurchases and a 10% dividend increase.
  • Andretta highlighted upgrades from Moody's and Fitch and positive outlooks from Moody's and S&P, stating, "Our focus on operational excellence and technology advancements was evident this year as we achieved our goal of delivering positive operating leverage."
  • CFO Perry Beberman reported, "Credit sales of $27.8 billion increased 3% year-over-year," and revenue increased $7 million, driven by pricing changes and paper statement fees. He added, "Total noninterest expenses decreased $72 million or 3%, driven by a $43 million lower year-over-year net impact from debt repurchases."

Outlook

  • Bread Financial expects full year 2026 average credit card and other loans growth to be "up low single digits compared to 2025" and anticipates "total revenue growth...up low single digits, largely in line with average loan growth." Net interest margin is forecasted to be "near to slightly above the full year 2025 rate."
  • Beberman projected a "year-over-year net loss rate in the 7.2% to 7.4% range for 2026." The company expects the normalized effective tax rate to be in the 25% to 27% range and targets a "longer-term mid-20% ROTCE."

Financial Results

  • Net income available to common stockholders was $53 million. Adjusted net income and earnings per diluted share were $95 million and $2.07, respectively. Tangible book value per common share grew 23% year-over-year to $57.57.
  • Credit sales for the quarter reached $8.1 billion, a 2% increase year-over-year. Average loans were $18.0 billion, and end-of-period loans were $18.8 billion, nearly flat year-over-year.
  • Revenue increased $49 million, or 5%, primarily due to pricing changes. Adjusted total noninterest expenses decreased $25 million, or 5%, when excluding debt repurchase impacts.
  • Net interest margin was 18.9%. The fourth quarter reserve rate was 11.2% and net loss rate was 7.4%. Liquidity position ended at $6.0 billion in liquid assets and undrawn credit facilities.

Q&A

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: Asked about drivers of loan growth and Bread Pay's outlook. Andretta responded, "We are underwriting the way we've always underwritten...no general loosening. It's a gradual look as credit improves." Bread Pay volume is expected to pick up with new partners.
  • Sakhrani followed up on credit quality amid layoff headlines. Beberman replied, "We are encouraged by what we're seeing in our underlying data...our early entry rate...is now below the pre-pandemic levels."
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: Inquired about T&E product growth. Andretta described a broad product array, highlighting the enhanced Caesars Rewards card and strong T&E spend, especially AAA. Orenbuch also asked about NIM components; Beberman noted stability with "continued lower billed late fees as delinquency improves."
  • John Hecht, Jefferies: Asked about DTC deposits. Beberman said the long-term goal is "probably 70% plus of our portfolio," and pricing is competitive.
  • Mihir Bhatia, BofA: Asked if returning to a 6% net loss rate is a priority. Beberman explained it's a priority "but not force it there," stressing profit-oriented underwriting.
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked about operating leverage. Beberman expressed confidence in delivering positive operating leverage in 2026, with magnitude depending on macro conditions.
  • Reginald Smith, JPMorgan: Asked about personal loans and AI impact. Andretta noted personal loans are a small, gradually growing part of the portfolio. Beberman detailed over 200 machine learning models and 60+ AI initiatives in motion.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts generally maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, probing for details on loan growth, credit quality, and NIM, with some caution about underlying consumer risks and macro uncertainty.
  • Management's prepared remarks reflected confidence and a constructive outlook, using phrases such as "we are pleased with our 2025 financial and operational results and remain confident in our ability to generate returns." During Q&A, management maintained a measured and disciplined stance, reiterating prudent underwriting and gradual improvement. Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remained confident but became more focused on operational discipline and technology innovation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from flat loan growth in Q3 to low single-digit growth for 2026. Management emphasized product innovation and new partner signings in both quarters, but Q4 highlighted a broader digital and AI-driven operational transformation.
  • Tangible book value, net income, and return metrics moderated sequentially, reflecting seasonal impacts and higher transaction volumes tied to the holiday period. The Q4 call placed stronger emphasis on operational leverage, technology investment, and capital allocation, with increased details on AI initiatives and the evolving product mix.
  • Analysts in Q4 were more focused on sustainable loan growth, technology impacts, and deposit funding, compared to prior quarter's focus on credit tightening and consumer health stability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macroeconomic risks, including inflation and interest rate decreases, which could pressure net interest income and margin. Beberman explained, "Our weightings remained unchanged again this quarter...reserve rate typically increases sequentially in the first quarter as holiday transactor balances pay down."
  • Analysts flagged uncertainty on consumer spending, possible layoffs, and deposit competition. Management reiterated prudent risk modeling and dynamic underwriting as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Bread Financial closed 2025 with steady operational and credit improvements, expanded product offerings, and a renewed partner base. Management projects low single-digit loan and revenue growth for 2026, supported by responsible underwriting and ongoing investment in technology, including AI-driven initiatives. The company remains focused on operational efficiency, capital optimization, and maintaining resilient credit metrics as it targets further growth and value creation in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: National Fuel Gas outlines $7.60–$8.10 EPS target for 2026 while advancing Ohio utility acquisition and pipeline expansions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: National Fuel Gas Company (NFG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • David Bauer, President and CEO, opened the call by recognizing the operations team for maintaining system reliability during challenging winter conditions and reported “adjusted earnings per share of $2.06, right in line with our expectations.” Bauer highlighted a “29% increase in adjusted EBITDA compared to the prior year” in the integrated upstream and gathering segment, with strong regulated business performance driven by the New York utility's 3-year rate settlement and the pipeline modernization tracker in Pennsylvania.
  • Bauer stated the outlook for natural gas “is as strong as it’s ever been with demand at all-time highs,” and emphasized expansion in Seneca’s inventory and a 30% capital efficiency gain since 2023. “Well results from our Lower Utica program in Tioga County remain among the basin’s best,” and Upper Utica delineation has “essentially doubled our core Tioga inventory estimate.”
  • Bauer reported on pipeline expansion: “The Tioga Pathway project is moving forward according to schedule...our Shippingport Lateral Project has now received all its required permits, keeping it on track for a late calendar 2026 in-service date.” He announced a pending Pennsylvania utility rate case for an “approximately $20 million increase in rates.”
  • Bauer confirmed progress on the CenterPoint Ohio LDC acquisition, stating, “we completed a well-executed $350 million private placement of common stock, which satisfies our equity need for the transaction.”
  • Timothy Silverstein, CFO, stated, “National Fuel had a great start to the fiscal year with adjusted EPS of $2.06, which keeps us on track to achieve our full year guidance.” Silverstein explained transaction and integration costs related to the Ohio utility acquisition and discussed permanent financing, noting “we expect to issue approximately $1.5 billion in long-term debt.”
  • Silverstein also reported, “Fiscal '26 adjusted EPS is projected to grow 14% over last year, and the setup for 2027 is for even more growth across the organization.”
  • Justin Loweth, President of National Fuel Gas Midstream Company and Seneca Resources, reported “net production was 109 Bcf, an increase of 12% over the first quarter of fiscal '25.” He reaffirmed “fiscal '26 guidance with production of 440 to 455 Bcf and capital of $560 million to $610 million.”

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed the “adjusted EPS guidance range of $7.60 to $8.10 or $7.85 at the midpoint” for fiscal 2026, with all previous assumptions unchanged.
  • Silverstein said, “We are seeing some tailwinds that could favorably impact full year results, particularly on our integrated upstream and gathering cost structure and in-basin prices.” He maintained the “previous $3.75 assumption for the remainder of the fiscal year” for natural gas pricing.
  • No changes to capital outlook or production guidance were announced compared to the previous quarter.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EPS for the quarter was $2.06.
  • Net production for the quarter was 109 Bcf, up 12% year-over-year.
  • The integrated upstream and gathering business delivered a 29% increase in adjusted EBITDA compared to the prior year.
  • The company completed a $350 million private placement of common stock in December related to the Ohio utility acquisition.
  • Silverstein stated, “We expect to achieve the low end of our previously disclosed 2.5 to 3x net debt-to-EBITDA range by the end of the first year post closing.”

Q&A

  • Zachary Parham, JPMorgan: Asked about the ability to benefit from local price spikes. Justin Loweth responded, “We do always keep open a little bit of gas daily...so absolutely...we do keep a small portion open to try to take advantage of those prices when they happen.”
  • Parham also inquired about future pipeline growth beyond Tioga Pathway and Line N Lateral. Bauer replied, “We are in active dialogue with other parties and fully believe we'll have additional opportunities down the road.”
  • Noah Hungness, BofA Securities: Asked about D&C costs for Gen 4 design and cost comparisons. Loweth explained, “The main cost...adds probably $150 to $175 a foot, something like that...we think there's a pretty meaningful uplift that is significantly in excess of that incremental cost.”
  • Margaret Drefke, Goldman Sachs: Asked about optimal production growth and the role of market pricing. Loweth stated, “Our base plan is to continue in that mid-digit range, kind of 3% to 7% per year on average.”
  • John Freeman, Raymond James: Questioned M&A focus post-CenterPoint. Bauer said, “I think the CenterPoint deal kind of rebalances the company a bit and it gives us the flexibility to look at transactions on both the regulated and nonregulated side.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but probing tone, focusing on flexibility in response to gas price volatility, project pipeline, and capital allocation. Their tone was neutral to slightly positive, reflecting interest in project execution and future growth opportunities.
  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks, with Bauer stating, “the outlook for natural gas is as strong as it’s ever been,” and Silverstein affirming guidance and discussing positive industry tailwinds. Responses in the Q&A remained factual and open, with occasional emphasis on ongoing evaluation and discipline.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst focus shifted more toward operational flexibility and capital allocation, while management sustained a confident outlook and reiterated growth projections.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remains unchanged, with reaffirmation of fiscal 2026 EPS and production targets.
  • Capital efficiency gains and inventory growth in the Utica program were updated, with a continued emphasis on expansion and cost control.
  • Management’s confidence and sentiment were consistent with the previous quarter, maintaining a positive outlook on growth and industry trends.
  • Analyst sentiment shifted slightly to emphasize market volatility and project execution.
  • Strategic focus continued on pipeline expansion, inventory growth, and the Ohio LDC acquisition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted natural gas price volatility as a key variable, with Silverstein stating, “Natural gas prices remain the biggest variable for our outlook.”
  • Integration and transaction costs related to the Ohio utility acquisition were noted, with an expectation that “a fair amount of the integration costs can be recovered in the future.”
  • Regulatory risks around rate cases and infrastructure development were discussed, with Bauer mentioning pending rate cases and policy shifts in New York and Ohio.
  • Analysts raised concerns about capital allocation, project execution timing, and market-driven production adjustments.

Final Takeaway

National Fuel Gas Company’s first quarter of fiscal 2026 delivered results in line with expectations, underpinned by higher production, improved capital efficiency, and the continued strength of its regulated and integrated businesses. The company reaffirmed its full-year guidance and remains focused on disciplined operational execution, pipeline expansions, and the successful integration of the pending Ohio utility acquisition. Management emphasized a robust outlook for natural gas demand, ongoing cost control, and a strategic approach to capital allocation, positioning the company for continued growth and value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AI disrupts traditional software business, ‘AI is eating software’ – analyst
data.description:
Infrastructure software companies (DTCR), (NQRPCI:IND) are outperforming as artificial intelligence disrupts traditional software businesses, according to Ben Reitzes, head of technology research at Melius Research.

Following IBM’s (IBM) latest earnings report, which showed sequential acceleration in software organic growth, Reitzes pointed to the company as a prime example of this shift. He maintains a buy rating on IBM (IBM) with a $390 price target.

“AI is eating software. You’re seeing it,” Reitzes said in an interview with CNBC. “This is what we’ve been saying for a couple of years now. And this is an infrastructure software company that has stable recurring revenue streams, and it accelerated sequentially.”

Reitzes drew a sharp contrast between infrastructure software companies and the struggling SaaS sector. He argued that companies “in the pipes and doing the data connections” will continue to outperform traditional software providers threatened by generative AI tools.

Regarding the disruption posed by AI models like Claude, Reitzes was blunt: “Something a kindergartner could look at and say that threatens software.”

The analyst’s $390 price target is based on enterprise value to free cash flow metrics. Reitzes argued that IBM (IBM) deserves a premium valuation compared to SaaS companies because its free cash flow is “higher quality” than competitors who rely on non-GAAP accounting while their “share count is going up to infinity because they’re giving out stock options.”

Reitzes also highlighted IBM’s (IBM) disciplined capital allocation, noting the company has “demonstrated they can use the free cash flow to do deals that add value to their infrastructure software franchise.”

He additionally pointed to IBM’s (IBM) quantum computing efforts as a long-term catalyst that isn’t yet reflected in the stock price.

“There is nothing to own in software except for IBM and a few others,” Reitzes concluded, reinforcing his view that the company stands as one of the few attractive options in a sector facing existential pressure from AI.

Software ETFs: (IGPT), (XSW), (AOTS)

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (WTAI), (XAIX), (WISE), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), (CHAT)

data.title: Trump says he'll name new Federal Reserve chair next week
data.description:
"We're going to be announcing next week sometime, I think, the head of the Fed," President Donald Trump said during a Cabinet meeting on Thursday. "It will be a person that I think will do a good job."

He once again decried that the Federal Reserve has kept interest rates too high. On Wednesday, the Federal Open Market Committee kept its benchmark policy rate at 3.50%-3.75%, saying that the U.S. economy is expanding solidly and there are signs that the labor market has stabilized.

"We're paying far too much interest in the Fed — the fed rate's too high, unacceptably high… We should have the lowest interest rate of anywhere in the world," Trump said.

He believes that it should be two points, "and even three points" lower than where it is today, he said. Reducing the federal funds rate target range by three percentage points would bring it down to 0.5%-0.75%.

"Each point is the equivalent of, I would say, $500B," he said, likely referring to the lower interest the U.S. would pay on its debt.  "So if you have two points, then you have a trillion dollars in savings." 

"It's so easy to do, but we have a guy who doesn't want to do it, because I think he's politically biased," Trump said. 

The Fed's mandate from Congress is to conduct monetary policy to maintain price stability and full employment. Fed Chair Jerome Powell has consistently maintained that political considerations aren't part of the decision-making process.

With the help of the Fed, "we could hit numbers that we've never hit before — we could hit eight, nine, ten (percent)," he said, referring to GDP growth. "There's  no reason why not."

"Growth is a great thing. It doesn't mean inflation," Trump said. "If inflation comes, we'll take care of it when it comes."

The president also discussed the importance of tariffs and the Supreme Court case, which has yet to issue its opinion on the legality of the tariffs Trump implemented using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

"I hope we're going to be successful with the Supreme Court case, because we're waiting," he said. "And we're taking in hundreds of billions of dollars, and we don't want to give it back."

The tariffs have "given us tremendous strength and national security," he said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
